coredata.dc:title: Detection of tartrazine in fake saffron containing products by a sensitive optical nanosensor
coredata.dc:creator: Zoughi, Sheida
Faridbod, Farnoush
Amiri, Amir
Ganjali, Mohammad Reza
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent assay for the selective analysis of tartrazine was developed. Tartrazine is a health-threatening food additive commonly used as fake saffron. An optical nanosensor was fabricated based on molecular imprinting technique in which carbon dots (CDs) as fluorophores and tartrazine as a template molecule were embedded in molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) matrix. The synthesized CDs embedded in MIP (CDs-MIP) was characterized by various methods. The fluorescence intensity of (CDs-MIP) was selectively quenched in the presence of tartrazine in comparison with other similar food color additives. The correlation between the quenching of CD-MIP and the concentration of tartrazine was used as an optical sensing for rapid detection of tartrazine in the range of 3.3–20.0 nM (1.8–10.7 μg L−1) with detection limit of 1.3 nM (0.70 μg L−1). Eventually, the designed nanosensor was successfully applied for tartrazine detection in foodstuffs such as fake saffron, saffron tea and saffron ice cream samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tartrazine
Food color
Fluorescence nanosensor
Carbon dots
Molecularly imprinted polymer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs, CDs-MIP and CDs-NIP
Characterization of CDs, CDs-MIP and CDs-NIP
Interaction between tartrazine and the optical sensor
Optimization of parameters affecting the performance of nanosensor
The CDs-MIP nanosensoranalytical performance in the determination of tartrazine
Study of repeatability, reproducibility, and stability of designed nanosensor
Analysis of tartrazine in real samples
Comparing the proposed nanosensor with previously optical methods reported

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs; TEM image (A), SAED (B), UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra (C), FT-IR (D), and XRD (E).

FT-IR spectra of CDs (black), CDs-APTES (green) and CDs-MIP (red) (A), TEM images of CDs-MIP (B) and CDs-NIP (C), SEM images of CDs-MIP (D) and CDs-NIP (E). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
XPS (A) and EDX images of CDs-MIP (B).

Effect of pH (A) and effect of incubation time (B) on the fluorescence quenching of CDs-MIP; Fluorescence spectra of CDs-MIP (C) and CDs-NIP (D) in the presence of different concentrations of tartrazine and calibration curves (E); Determination of tartrazine and various potential interfering substances like similar components to tartrazine using CDs-MIP (F); effect of foreign co-existing ions on the fluorescence quenching of CDs-MIP (G). The CDs-MIP and CDs-NIP (1 g/L) dispersed in water and methanol with incubation time of 10 min in pH 7. The concentrations of various co-existing common ions are 10 nM. F0 and F are fluorescent intensities of CDs-MIP before and after adding the corresponding interfering substances The concentrations of tartrazine, sunset yellow, quinoline and yellow 2G are 10 nM. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Quantitative measurement of spiked tartrazine in saffron tea using the prepared nanosensor.
Comparison between the introduced nanosensor and the other reported optical fluorescence probes.

row.entry: Fluorescence probe based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Fluorescent probe based on N,Cl co-doped carbon dots
Fluorescence quenching method based on acriflavine
A fluorescent sensor based on carbon quantum dots

head.ce:title: Detection of tartrazine in fake saffron containing products by a sensitive optical nanosensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Chemistry Department, Center of Excellence in Electrochemistry, School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical Chemistry Department, Center of Excellence in Electrochemistry, School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biosensor Research Center, Endocrinology & Metabolism Molecular-Cellular Sciences Institute, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Biosensor Research Center, Endocrinology & Metabolism Molecular-Cellular Sciences Institute, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent assay for the selective analysis of tartrazine was developed. Tartrazine is a health-threatening food additive commonly used as fake saffron. An optical nanosensor was fabricated based on molecular imprinting technique in which carbon dots (CDs) as fluorophores and tartrazine as a template molecule were embedded in molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) matrix. The synthesized CDs embedded in MIP (CDs-MIP) was characterized by various methods. The fluorescence intensity of (CDs-MIP) was selectively quenched in the presence of tartrazine in comparison with other similar food color additives. The correlation between the quenching of CD-MIP and the concentration of tartrazine was used as an optical sensing for rapid detection of tartrazine in the range of 3.3–20.0 nM (1.8–10.7 μg L−1) with detection limit of 1.3 nM (0.70 μg L−1). Eventually, the designed nanosensor was successfully applied for tartrazine detection in foodstuffs such as fake saffron, saffron tea and saffron ice cream samples.

ce:keywords.ce:section-title: Chemical compounds studied in this article

ce:section.ce:para: Analyses of food and cosmetics unauthorized additive is of great importance. Using some of food additives can cause various health problems (Ameur et al., 2020). Tartrazine (E102) (trisodium1-(4-sulfonatophenyl)-4-(4-sulfonatophenylazo)-5-pyrazolone-3-carboxylate)) is a lemon yellow azo dye. It is previously used as a synthetic food color (Jain & Mathur, 2015) in the food industry in fruit juice, candies, jellies, jams, flavored chips, cakes, ice cream, sauces, and cereals (Rovina, Siddiquee, & Shaarani, 2017) which still is used in some countries.

In human body, azo dyes are normally removed by kidneys and liver (Lei et al., 2013). However, some studies have shown that tartrazine may cause various health effects such as liver disorders, some types of cancers, generative toxicity, kidney problems, neurobehavioral poisonousness, hyperactivities in children, asthma, and allergy (Gupta et al., 2019). Regarding the mentioned adverse health effects, tartrazine usage is confined in many countries (Ameur et al., 2020).

Saffron is one the most expensive spice in the world and it is believed that it originated from Iran. To date, Iran is one the leading producer of saffron. In recent years, using tartrazine as a fake substitute for saffron has been increasing, leading to dire consequences.

According to the above mentioned, developing and fabricating sensitive and selective sensors for simple and rapid detection of tartrazine is demanded. Different instrumental such as spectrophotometric and chromatographic techniques, were reported for tartrazine analyses which was previously reviewed (Ntrallou, Gika, & Tsochatzis, 2020). Also, capillary electrophoresis (Zhang et al., 2014), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Al Shamari, Wabaidur, Alwarthan, Khan, & Siddiqui, 2020), were used for tartrazine determination. Although these methods are very sensitive and accurate, suffer from several disadvantages such as expert operatory, time-consuming, solvent-usage-intensive, and high cost (Masteri-Farahani, Mashhadi-Ramezani, & Mosleh, 2020) which make them not to be appropriate for portable and rapid analysis. Besides, some optical and electrochemical techniques sensors have been reported for fast detection of tartrazine too. Voltammetry based on graphene and mesoporous TiO2 modified carbon paste electrode (Gan, Sun, Meng, Song, & Zhang, 2013), voltammetry based on the GN-Ni nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode (Gan et al., 2013), and graphene layer-wrapped phosphotungstic acid (PTA) hybrid on the surface of glassy carbon electrode (Gan et al., 2012) are applied to study of tartrazine level in different samples. Optical sensors will be compared to this developed nanosensor later.

To develop aselective and rapidmethod, fluorescence spectroscopy using carbon dots (CDs) and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) technique was recently applied for detection as nanosensors (Ahmadi, Faridbod, & Mehrzad-Samarin, 2019; Mehrzad-Samarin, Faridbod, & Ganjali, 2019; Mehrzad-Samarin, Faridbod, Dezfuli, & Ganjali, 2017). Recently, carbon dots (CDs), a new category of fluorescent nanomaterials has gained great attention in fabricating optical sensors due to their advantages such as water-solubility, eco-friendliness, low toxicity, magnificent photo-stability, wide absorption spectrum, symmetrical emission, superior biocompatibility, simple synthesis, high sensitivity, and wide linear range (Shen, Lou, Liu, Dong, & Shan, 2020). These significant advantages make them an excellent choice to be applied for fabricating fluorescent probes (Mehrzad-Samarin et al., 2017).

However, these applications are limited owing to the low selectivity of CDs upon analytes (Xiao, Su, Teng, Hao, & Bi, 2020). Thus, exploiting a scaffold to enhance the selectivity of the CDs would boost their benefit in sensor and biosensor design (Wang et al., 2019). Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are synthesized based on the polymerization of a selective matrix in the presence of template molecules which leads to achieving the MIP with predetermined recognition ability, selective binding, low production cost, and good physical thermal stability (Mabrouk, Hammad, Abdella, & Mansour, 2020; Ran, Lin, Yao, Hong, & Huang, 2019; Xu et al., 2020). Polymerization of acrylic polymers or organic acrylates was used for the fabrication of traditional MIPs whichis not applicable for a fluorescent detectiondue to their absorptions. To overcome these drawbacks, inorganic matrices, such as siloxane-based MIPs can be replaced in a hopeful manner. These materials are produced through hydrolysis and condensation silane monomers in basic or acidic conditions (Gan et al., 2020). These novel MIPs have several advantages such as higher porosity, surface area, chemical inertness, change upon exposure to organic solvents, and optical transparency in UV–Vis (Ahmadi et al., 2019; Gan et al., 2018).
Employing fluorescent carbon dots embedded in silica based molecularly imprinted polymer as a nanosensor for tartrazine rapid detection is the aim of this study.

Tartrazine, citric acid, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), n-hexane, absolute ethanol, and methanol were obtained from Merck Chemical Co. (Germany). Concentrated acetic acid, phosphoric acid 85%, boric acid, hydrochloric acid, ammonium hydroxide, ammonium fluoride, and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (USA). All solutions were prepared in double-distilled water. A stock solution of tartrazine (0.001 M) was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. Rests of solutions were prepared by appropriate dilution of stock solution. Britton-Robinson buffer solution (0.04 M and pH 5–9) was prepared from concentrated acetic acid, boric acid, and phosphoric acid 85%.

A perkin-Elmer FT-IR spectrometer was used for performing spectroscopic experiments. Structure of nanocomposites (CDs-MIP and CDs-NIP) were studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany, HR-TEM). Morphology of nanocomposites was characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, FEINOVA NANOSEM 450). Fluorescence spectra were recorded using perkin-Elmer luminescence spectrometer Fluorescence (LS50). UV–Vis absorption was recorded by a Perkin-Elmer (Lambda 25) UV–Vis spectrometer. XRD pattern of CDs was studied by D8-Advance (Bruker AXS). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was done by Bes-Tech device (Germany). The energy dispersive X-Ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping was carried out by MIRA III (SAMX detector). The HPLC analysis was done using Agilent instrument with C-18 column. The pH values were recorded using a Micronal B474 pH meter equipped.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs, CDs-MIP and CDs-NIP
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized through previously optimized thermal pyrolysis method (Dong et al., 2012). To prepare CD nanoparticles, the optimized amount of citric acid powder 2.00 g heated at 180 °C (Ahmadi et al., 2019). Heating was stopped when the solution color turned yellow, and the solution was cooled to room temperature. The CDs were characterized by TEM, XRD, UV–Vis, fluorescence spectroscopy, and FT-IR. In the next step, 5 ml APTES as an optimized amount was added to CDs (Mehrzad-Samarin et al., 2017), and the mixture was stirred and heated at 35 °C till a viscose compound formed (about 40 min.). According to the number of cavities in polymer formed by the template molecule, as well as easy washing and extraction of the template molecule, 0.01 g tartrazine was dissolved in 2 ml water and methanol mixture (2:5), then half of this solution was added to the mixture of APTES-CDs. Next, optimized amounts of TEOS (1 ml) and catalyst solution (60 μL) made from 0.018 g NH4F and 2.3 ml NH4OH in 10 ml water, were added respectively and the mixture was stirred for 60 min. After 12 h, the newly synthesized CDs-MIP was centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 10 min and then CDs-MIP nanocomposite was washed several times by a mixture of deionized water and methanol (2:5) to remove template molecules and non-reacted materials. In each washing step, the polymer was mixed with the solvent in a tube and centrifuged, then the supernatant solution was taken and analyzed by UV–Vis. Finally, the tartrazine molecules were removed and the peak of tartrazine in the UV–Vis region was completely disappeared (Fig. 1 supplementary). Also, CDs-NIP was synthesized under the same method in the lack of tartrazine as template.

In order to prepare the desired fluorescent probe, 0.1 g of dried CDs-MIP powder was transferred to a 100 ml-volumetric flask containing buffer, while was sonicated for 2 h. The solution was than cooled at room temperature. For each test, 10 µL of the CDs-MIP solution was transferred into a 4.5 ml-fluorescent quartz cuvette containing 3 ml BR buffer solution. The CDs-MIP solution was stored in 4 °C for further experiments.

Samples such as fake saffron, saffron tea bags, and saffron ice cream samples were purchased from a local supermarket in Tehran (Iran). The fake saffron sample was prepared by dissolving 0.1 g fake saffron powder in 2 ml double distilled water. Different concentrations were obtained by appropriate dilution of stock solution.
To prepare tea sample, the tea bag was immersed in 2 ml distilled hot water for 10 min, and tea extraction was passed through a filter several times.

0.20 g saffron ice cream was weighted and dissolved in 5 ml of methanolic KOH (a mixture of KOH and ethanol 95%) in a tube. The tube was capped tightly and vortexed for 15 s. Lower half of tube was immersed in a hot bath (80 °c) for totally 15 min during which the sample was vortexed for 10 s every 5 min. The tube was cooled down with tap water, and 1 ml of distilled water and 5 ml of n-hexane were added to the tube. The tube was vortexed for 1 min and centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 1 min. The supernatant was separated for the following analysis. The adequate volume of the prepared real sample was used for the analysis of tartrazine (Martin, Oberson, Meschiari, & Munari, 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs, CDs-MIP and CDs-NIP
ce:section.ce:para: According to the TEM image of CDs, the size of nanoparticles was smaller than 10 nm with a sphere shape (Fig. 1 A). The selected area (electron) diffraction (SAED) pattern shows diffused rings indicating the polycrystalline nature of the synthesized carbon dots (Fig. 1B) (De & Karak, 2013). The UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra confirmed the synthesis of CDs, with a band from 300 nm to 360 nm, validating the absorption edge of CDs and a symmetric fluorescence emission at 415 nm, respectively (Fig. 1C). FT-IR analysis of CDs proved the expected CDs structure as a peak at 1712 cm−1 (CO stretching), and a broadband between 3300 and 3500 cm−1 (hydroxyl groups) suggests the presence of carbonyl and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface and edges of CDs (Fig. 1D). The XRD characterization showed in Fig. 1E is completely consistent with other references (De & Karak, 2013).

The FT-IR spectrum was obtained to investigate the interactions between CDs and APTES (Fig. 2 A). The peak at 1712 cm−1 is due to carbonyl groups and also a broadband from 3300 to 3500 cm−1 has appeared because of the hydroxyl groups of CDs (Fig. 2A black). In the FT-IR spectrum of CDs-APTES two strong peaks around 1078 cm−1 and 1106 cm−1, confirmed covalent SiOC and Si-O-Si interactions respectively. A peak at 1712 cm−1 related to the carboxyl group was eliminated, and a peak at 1605 cm−1 was appeared due to the amine group in APTES. These changes indicated the possibility of interaction between the amine groups with the carboxylic groups leading to NH3 + and COO− formation. The peaks at 3300 and 3350 cm−1 represented stretching of the NH bond related to the existing amine group in APTES. The mentioned data show many free amine groups from ATPES to interact with functional groups of tartrazine (Fig. 2A green). In the FT-IR spectrum of CDs-MIP (Fig. 2A red), polymerization completion was confirmed based on peaks which were stronger than those in the FT-IR spectrum of CDs-APTES.

The TEM technique was used to obtain the size and morphology of CDs-MIP. The particle size of CDs was around 10 nm based on the TEM image. The brighter color of CDs-MIP compared to the CDs-NIP was due to existent empty spaces belonging to tartrazine molecules (Fig. 2B and C). The SEM technique was performed to study the surface structure of CDs-MIP and the CDs-NIP. Based on SEM images, the surface of CDs-MIP was globular (Fig. 2D). Also, the existence of imprinted cavities on CDs-MIP increased the roughness of the surface. The surface of CDs-NIP in the SEM images (Fig. 2E) was smoother without specific sites related to the template molecules. Fig. 3 A, shows the XPS spectra of CDs-MIP. The O 1s peak at binding energy around 533.00 eV indicates oxygen binding to carbon and silicon. These XPS results verify the existence of silicon dioxide, which have been discussed for the FT-IR results earlier in this section as well as hydroxyl groups on the edge and surface of CDs. The N 1s detail peak can be fitted here with one peak at binding energy of 400.00 eV corresponds to nitrogen binding to carbon related to amine groups presence in silica polymer. The C 1s detail peak can be fitted here with three peaks at binding energy of 284.00 eV, 284.8 eV, and 286 eV related to CC, CC, CO, and CN binding. These XPS results proved carbon binding to carbon and oxygen in CDs structure and carbon binding to carbon and nitrogen in MIP structure. The Si 2p detail peak can be fitted with two peaks at binding energy of 103.00 eV, and 104.00 eV, mostly related to the silica polymer structure correspond to silica binding to bound carbon to oxygen and silica as a bridge between oxygens, respectively. In contrast to the metal particles, no Si–OH bonding can be identified in both spectra. The elemental composition of CDs-MIP nanocomposite was identified and quantified by the EDX mapping. As shown in Fig. 3B, elements are well distributed in the CDs-MIP nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between tartrazine and the optical sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The mechanism for the fluorescence quenching of CDs-MIP is based on the interaction of tartrazine molecule and functional cavities. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of tartrazine and fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs-MIP were studied (Fig. 2 supplementary). Since there is complete overlap between them, the fluorescence quenching mechanism might be a kind of energy transfer and supposed to be Forster Resonance Energy transfer (FRET) (Yang, Luo, Tan, Jia, & Liao, 2017). The Stern-Volmer equation can be used to quantify the fluorescence quenching of CDs-MIP (Wei et al., 2015):
(1)
F 0
/ F
=
1
+
K
sv
Q

In Stern-Volmer equation, F and F0 are defined as the fluorescence intensity in the presence of quencher and lack of quencher, respectively. Ksv is constant of quenching, and [Q] is the concentration of the quencher.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of parameters affecting the performance of nanosensor

ce:section.ce:para: The influence of pH on fluorescence intensity of the CDs-MIP nanocomposite was studied over a range of 5.0–9.0. In this regard, 0.1 g CDs-MIP was dispersed in 100 ml BR buffer for 2 h. The concentration of tartrazine was the same for all of the measurements. Response intensity of the nanosensor was decreased in basic (pH higher than 8.0) and acidic (pH lower than 7.0) medium due to reduction of interactions between empty sites and tartrazine molecules in basic medium and disturbance of hydrogen bonding in acidic one, respectively. In pH values more than 7, probably because of the ionization of the silica layer that leads to surface defects, the amount of the quenching decreased (Chullasat, Nurerk, Kanatharana, Davis, & Bunkoed, 2018). Also, the intensity of response decreased when pH was lower than 7 most due to dissolving the active site of the composites during which the template molecules are not able to interact with recognition sites (Hou et al., 2016). Thus, neutral pH (7.0) was selected as the optimal pH for all measurements (Fig. 4 A).

The optimized time needed for effective adsorption of tartrazine on the CDs-MIP nanocomposite was monitored through quenching the fluorescence intensity. The optimized conditions were applied for response time study (pH of 7.0, the tartrazine concentration of 10 nM, and the temperature of 25 ± 1 °C). The time was applied over the range of 0 to 15 min. Maximum quenching obtained after 10 min, and this value was selected as an optimized incubation time in the analysis of tartrazine (Fig. 4B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The CDs-MIP nanosensoranalytical performance in the determination of tartrazine
ce:section.ce:para: Using optimal conditions, performance of the proposed nanosensor was investigated in analytical purposes. The fluorescence spectra of CDs-MIP and CDs-NIP was recorded in the presence of different concentrations of tartrazine as calibration series (Fig. 4C and D). The newly synthesized CDs-MIP showed a remarkable response in a linear range from 3.3 to 20.0 nM and LOD of 1.3 nM (Fig. 4E). The determination coefficient (R2) was 0.996 and 0.993 for CDs-MIP and CDs-NIP, respectively and the slope of the calibration curve for CDs-MIP was more than CDs-NIP indicating more sensitivity of CDs-MIP. Regarding low LOD, the proposed optical nanosensor was efficient and sensitive enough to trace analysis of tartrazine. The fluorescence measurements of each concentration of tartrazine were repeated five times and the relative standard deviation was obtained less than 3.3%.

The selectivity of the designed nanosensor, which is important for tartrazine measurement, was also evaluated. The compounds such as sunset yellow, quinoline, and yellow 2G, with color and chemical structures close to the tartrazine, were selected (Fig. 4F). During the synthesis of CDs-MIP, many specific identification sites were created on imprinted polymer for tartrazine as the template molecule which were similar to tartrazine molecule in terms of size, shape, and spatial arrangement. Therefore, tartrazine molecules were more appropriate than their analogs to occupying binding sites and quenching in fluorescence intensity was more drastic for tartrazine. In the case of CDs-NIP, the amounts of quenching in fluorescence intensity for tartrazine and similar compounds were negligible, which illustrates that tartrazine does not bind effectively to CDs-NIP. To find the effect of co-existing ions on the performance of the designed nanosensor, tartrazine was measured in the presence of various ions (Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, K+, Na+, Zn2+, NO3 −, Cl−). As shown in Fig. 4G, the presence of foreign ions in the solution had no significant effect on fluorescence quenching of the fluorescence probe during the detection of tartrazine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of repeatability, reproducibility, and stability of designed nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: For repeatability of the method, a test solution of tartrazine (10 nM) was determined for five times by a nanosensor. The RSD of this measurement was 3.2%.
Furthermore, 5 replicate synthesized nanosensors were prepared and the concentration of a test solution of tartrazine (10 nM) was measured with each of them. The RSD of all measurements was 4.3%.

Also, the stability of the materials (CDs-MIP) used in the proposed optical nanosensor was studied by recording the fluorescence emission of the CD-MIP up to five months. After three months, RSD was 4.5%, and in the period of three to five months, it was 5.3%. During this period of time, a solution of (0.1 ng/mL) of CDs-MIP was prepared for each measurement. Naturally, the results of this study also depend on how the nanocomposite powder was stored during this period. If the CDs-MIP powder is kept in a dry place without exposure to air in the refrigerator, the nanocomposite can be stable for up to five months.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of tartrazine in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Some commercial food samples in which most fake saffron (use of tartrazine) has been reported e.g., saffron powder, saffron ice cream, and saffron tea were analyzed by the proposed method through standard addition.

For saffron ice cream and fake saffron as real samples, the related fluorescence spectra are shown in Fig. 3 supplementary. Weight percentages (W/W %) of tartrazine in samples were 0.15% ± 0.04 and 0.11% ± 0.03 for fake saffron and saffron ice cream, respectively (n = 5). To validate the nanosensor performance, the obtained results were compared to the results obtained from the standard HPLC method. The results from HPLC analysis were 0.10 ± 0.02 and 0.08 ± 0.01 W/W% for fake saffron and saffron ice cream, respectively. Based on the statistical t-test with 95% confidence level there was no significant difference between the nanosensor and HPLC analysis results as t-experimental for fake saffron and saffron ice cream was 2.50 and 2.12, respectively (t-theoretical = 2.77). Furthermore, during the experiments, the tea bag sample contained no tartrazine, both using the nanosensor and according to HPLC method. To further verify the prepared optical nanosensor, we manually spiked different concentrations of tartrazine to the tea bag sample. The recovered concentrations of the spiked samples using the nanosensor are shown in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparing the proposed nanosensor with previously optical methods reported
ce:section.ce:para: A comparison of different optical methods has shown in Table 2 . As shown, the proposed nanosensor has improved LOD and practical linear range compared to previously reported ones. Due to remarkable properties, this method can be used for trace analysis of tartrazine in edible samples. This method also gives a relatively good selectivity compared to the other optical methods presented in Table 2, and there will be no need for an expert to work with the designed nanosensor.

In this study, a sensitive optical nanosensor was introduced for the detection of tartrazine based on CDs-MIP. Tartrazine can cause various health problems; hence, sensitive detection of tartrazine in food samples has great importance. The proposed optical nanosensor is an appropriate probe to detect the tartrazine level. The CDs-MIP was prepared using the reaction of APTES and TEOS with CD nanoparticles in the presence of the template molecules. This newly introduced fluorescent nanosensor was used to detect tartrazine level with low detection limit (1.3 nM), practical linear range (3.3–20.0 nM), strong fluorescence intensity and less interference. To verify the fabricated sensor, this probe was applied for the analysis of tartrazine in food samples. The facility, cost-effectiveness, lower toxicity and less interference will motivate scientists to use this method. Also, due to the simplicity of this technique, it can be used as chips based on the Lateral Flow technique for analysis of food materials. Finally, the proposed nanocomposite is practical sensing probe that could be modified and extended to analysis of other important targets accurately.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the University of Tehran. The authors thank Mr. Ehsan Hamzehpoor and Mrs. Yeganeh Habibi, PhD students in McGill University, for their help in this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.129197.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile optosensing protocol based on molecularly imprinted polymer coated on CdTe quantum dots for highly sensitive and selective amoxicillin detection
